feature_name: Dual-mode operation: Root Complex and End Point
description: The PCIe subsystem can operate as either a Root Complex (RC) or an End Point (EP). Mode selection is controlled by DEVSTAT.PCIESSMODE[1:0] or bootstrap configuration pins. This enables the device to enumerate downstream devices as RC or attach to a host as EP. Only a single function is supported in EP mode. Mode must be selected before link training and system discovery.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 1 (Features); Chapter 2.11 Operations; Local Config DEV_STAT_CTRL/DEV_STAT (0x1078)
dependencies: Power domain enabled via PSC; Proper REFCLK; DEVSTAT.PCIESSMODE set prior to LTSSM_EN
Remarks: Changing modes requires re-initialization; choose RC/EP based on system role

feature_name: Link width and speed: x1/x2, Gen1/Gen2 (2.5/5.0 GT/s)
description: The link supports one or two lanes and trains to Gen1 or Gen2 speeds. Dynamic width conversion allows operation when fewer lanes are available. Gen2 parameters are managed in Port Logic registers. Speed and width are negotiated during LTSSM link training. Throughput scales with lanes and generation within negotiated limits.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 1 (Overview); Port Logic PL_GEN2 register
dependencies: Proper SerDes PLL configuration; Valid REFCLK; Partner capability negotiation
Remarks: Use common clock mode for optimal jitter and stability when available

feature_name: Automatic credit management, ECRC, and AER
description: The core autonomously manages flow control credits to prevent buffer overrun. End-to-end CRC (ECRC) generation and checking can be enabled for integrity. Advanced Error Reporting (AER) surfaces detailed protocol errors. Software can monitor error status via capability and port logic registers. These mechanisms improve robustness under heavy traffic and error conditions.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 1 (Features); Chapter 3.7 PCIe Capabilities/AER
dependencies: Enable/monitor AER and ECRC per capability controls; Interrupt routing for error events
Remarks: Ensure system software logs and services AER to avoid silent failures

feature_name: Lane polarity inversion, lane reversal, dynamic width conversion
description: The PHY supports automatic receive polarity inversion to tolerate board swaps. Lane reversal is handled to accommodate flipped lane ordering on the PCB. Dynamic width conversion allows training to reduced width if not all lanes are present or healthy. These features increase link bring-up resilience across platforms. Software should monitor negotiated width to apply performance policies.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 1 (Features)
dependencies: SerDes configured and LTSSM enabled; Compatible partner link training
Remarks: Read negotiated width from LINK_STAT_CTRL and adjust DMA/QoS accordingly

feature_name: Configurable BARs and configuration/I-O filtering
description: BAR registers define inbound addressable regions for EP or bridge windows for RC. Configuration and I/O TLP filtering ensures only valid accesses are forwarded. BAR mask registers allow sizing and type control including 64-bit pairs. Proper configuration is required before exposing the device to enumeration. Misconfigured BARs can cause decode errors or timeouts.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 3.3/3.4 (Type0/Type1 BARs); Chapter 2.7 Address Translation
dependencies: Program BARx and masks; Set inbound windows via IB_*; Ensure non-cacheable mapping
Remarks: Lock down BARs before LTSSM enable in EP mode to avoid host probing faults

feature_name: Completion lookup and completion timeout handling
description: The core implements completion lookup to match requests and completions. Completion timeout support is exposed through Device Capabilities 2 registers. Timeouts can generate error status and optionally interrupts. Software policy should define retry or reset behavior on timeout. Proper configuration avoids false positives during long-latency operations.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 3.7 (DEV_CAP2 Completion Timeout); Chapter 2.16 Error Handling
dependencies: Enable relevant error/interrupt bits; Stable clocking; Correct MMR programming
Remarks: Tune timeout values based on topology and bridge depth

feature_name: Local config and application MMR access via BAR0/config window
description: Software can access local configuration space and application registers through a mapped window. BAR0 is typically used for application MMR exposure in EP mode. In RC mode the local config space is accessible via the internal window at a fixed offset. Only 32-bit non-burst accesses are supported for these apertures. Non-cacheable mappings are required to ensure protocol compliance.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 2.8 (Address Spaces) and Chapter 3.1 (Application Registers); Local Config base at 0x1000 offset
dependencies: Map BAR0 and local config window; MMU/MPU set to non-cacheable; 32-bit access only
Remarks: Avoid speculative loads; guard windows until link is stable

feature_name: Legacy INTx support (generate/receive)
description: The subsystem can generate legacy INTx in EP mode and receive INTx in RC mode. Status and enable bits exist per legacy line. Events are acknowledged via the IRQ_EOI mechanism. INTx is useful for compatibility with hosts lacking MSI. MSI is preferred for performance and scalability where available.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 2.14 Interrupt Support; Legacy INT STATUS/ENABLE at 0x180..0x1DC; IRQ_EOI at 0x050
dependencies: Interrupt controller integration; Proper masking and EOI write sequencing
Remarks: Favor MSI when supported; keep INTx as fallback

feature_name: Message Signaled Interrupts (MSI) with grouped vectors
description: The device supports MSI generation in EP mode and reception in RC mode. MSI vectors are grouped across MSI0..MSI7 blocks with W1S/W1C enable control. Each group controls a set of vectors allowing fine-grained enables. Status raw and masked views are provided for service. EOI writes finalize servicing per event model.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 2.14; MSI STATUS_RAW/STATUS/ENABLE_SET/CLR at 0x100..0x17C; IRQ_EOI at 0x050
dependencies: MSI capability programmed; Address/data written by RC; Vector masks configured
Remarks: Map MSI vectors to CPU IRQs carefully to avoid priority inversion

feature_name: PCIe power management and ASPM L0s/L1 (no L2)
description: The core implements PCI device power management states except D3cold with VAUX. Active State Power Management supports L0s and L1 link states autonomously when idle. L2 link state is not supported and should not be requested. RC software may transmit PM_TURNOFF; EP can signal PME. Software must coordinate with PSC power gating policies.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 2.15 Power Management; PMCMD at 0x020; PMCFG at 0x024
dependencies: PSC domain control; Capability bits set; Partner ASPM policy
Remarks: Ensure VAUX/D3cold flows are not assumed; honor L0s/L1 exit latency in drivers

feature_name: PHY loopback support (RC mode)
description: A PHY loopback mode is available to validate the SerDes data path. This helps isolate board-level issues during bring-up. Loopback is controlled via PHY/PCS registers. It is primarily intended for lab diagnostics, not runtime use. Link partner is not required during internal loopback tests.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 1 (Features); PCS/SerDes controls 0x380..0x394 (KeyStone I)
dependencies: SerDes configured and enabled; RC mode active; Test equipment as needed
Remarks: Disable loopback before normal traffic to avoid isolation from partner

feature_name: VBUSM master/slave 128-bit interfaces
description: The core exposes a 128-bit master port for inbound PCIe transactions to reach the internal bus. A 128-bit slave port accepts internal bus requests for outbound PCIe memory writes and reads. Proper arbitration and bandwidth planning are required on the SoC fabric. Endianness and privilege attributes travel with the requests. These ports are central to DMA-style data movement.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 2 (Architecture VBUSM interfaces)
dependencies: Functional SoC clocks; Bus fabric QoS; Non-cacheable mappings for PCIe windows
Remarks: Ensure AXI/SoC interconnect ordering rules align with PCIe semantics

feature_name: Endianness and byte-swap control for outbound traffic
description: An ENDIAN register allows selection of endianness and byte-swapping for outbound accesses. This supports integration with big-endian or little-endian system memory models. Misconfiguration leads to data corruption or unexpected ordering. Settings should be applied consistently with compiler and cache configuration. Verify with known patterns before enabling high-rate transfers.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 3.1 ENDIAN at 0x038
dependencies: Correct system endianness selection; Cache and MMU attributes coherent with choice
Remarks: Use a single endianness policy across all PCIe masters in the system

feature_name: Outbound address translation regions (OB)
description: Programmable outbound regions convert internal bus addresses to PCIe memory space. Each region is defined with low/high parts and an index selector. This enables mapping multiple discontiguous host apertures. Software must ensure non-overlapping, correctly sized windows. Incorrect mapping can cause accesses to hit unintended host memory.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 2.7; OB_OFFSET_INDEXn at 0x200+ and OB_OFFSETn_HI at 0x204+
dependencies: Program region base/size; Select region via index; Non-cacheable mappings upstream
Remarks: Keep a mapping table in the driver to reverse-translate for diagnostics

feature_name: Inbound BAR match windows (IB)
description: Inbound translation matches PCIe BARs to internal target address spaces. Start low/high and offset registers define the internal address window. Multiple BAR windows allow flexible placement of device resources. BAR permissions and sizes must align with host assignments. Proper setup is mandatory before exposing an EP to enumeration.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 2.7; IB_BARx/IB_STARTx_LO/HI/IB_OFFSETx at 0x300..0x33C
dependencies: BAR sizing/masks; Internal target address decode ready; Non-cacheable mapping
Remarks: Validate that BAR alignment matches host probing granularity

feature_name: I/O access window (RC-only, 4 KB)
description: A small I/O space aperture supports outbound I/O TLPs when acting as RC. The window base is programmed via IOBASE. Only 32-bit configuration-style accesses are permitted. This facilitates compatibility with legacy I/O devices. Usage is optional and often unnecessary on modern systems.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 2.8 (I/O Access Window); IOBASE at 0x00C
dependencies: RC mode; Partner devices that require I/O space; Proper decode downstream
Remarks: Prefer memory-mapped I/O; restrict I/O usage to legacy endpoints

feature_name: Access rules: 32-bit, non-burst, non-cacheable for windows
description: Application, local config, remote config, and I/O windows only allow 32-bit accesses. Bursting is disallowed to maintain protocol semantics. The regions must be mapped as non-cacheable to avoid speculation and reordering. Violating these rules can lead to hangs or silent data corruption. Drivers must enforce access widths and attributes.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 2.8 (Access rules)
dependencies: MMU/MPU attributes; Bus master configuration; Compiler barriers in driver
Remarks: Use readl/writel-style primitives that enforce width and ordering

feature_name: Clocking and common clock configuration
description: The SerDes relies on a differential REFCLK, typically 100 MHz with SSC support. A common clock architecture with the partner is strongly recommended. LINK_STAT_CTRL includes COMMON_CLK_CFG to declare common or independent clocking. Correct declaration improves jitter tolerance and timing margins. Mismatch can cause link instability or reduced speed.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 2.2 (Clock Control); LINK_STAT_CTRL at 0x1080
dependencies: Stable REFCLK; Correct COMMON_CLK_CFG; Matching partner setting
Remarks: Re-train link after changing COMMON_CLK_CFG to apply new timing

feature_name: PSC power domain and mode bootstrap
description: The PCIe module resides in a power domain controlled by the PSC. A bootstrap signal (PCIESSEN) determines initial enable state. Software can override and select RC/EP mode via DEVSTAT registers. Proper sequencing ensures that clocks and power are valid before register access. Incorrect sequencing may require a full reset to recover.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 2 (Power and mode); DEVSTAT fields; Device data manual PSC references
dependencies: PSC enable; Clock domain active; DEVSTAT configured
Remarks: Integrate PSC sequencing into system power manager

feature_name: Reset support and downstream hot reset (RSTCMD.INIT_RST)
description: Conventional PCIe cold and warm resets are supported by the core. Software can request a downstream hot reset sequence using RSTCMD.INIT_RST. Reset ordering must account for SerDes PLL and link partner readiness. Interrupts and DMA must be quiesced before reset. Proper reset handling enables clean recovery after errors.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 3.1 RSTCMD at 0x014; Chapter 2 (Reset)
dependencies: RSTCMD write permissions; Link idled; Error handlers armed
Remarks: Log reason codes before issuing resets for post-mortem analysis

feature_name: SerDes PLL configuration and lock monitoring
description: The SerDes PLL must be configured for the desired line rate multiplier. Software sets MPY and ENPLL in PCIE_SERDES_CFGPLL. Lock status is read from PCIE_SERDES_STS.LOCK before enabling link training. Per-lane status bits indicate RX/TX readiness. Correct PLL programming is prerequisite to stable link training.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 2.3 (KeyStone I SerDes); PCIE_SERDES_CFGPLL 0x02620358; PCIE_SERDES_STS 0x0262015C
dependencies: Valid REFCLK; Correct MPY selection; ENPLL asserted and LOCK achieved
Remarks: Record refclk/MPY pairing in board file to guard against misconfigurations

feature_name: LTSSM control via CMD_STATUS[LTSSM_EN]
description: Link training is initiated by setting LTSSM_EN in CMD_STATUS. Training progresses through states visible in DEBUG registers. Drivers should poll until L0 is reached before enabling traffic. Retraining may be requested after policy changes. Failing to reach L0 indicates SerDes or partner issues requiring diagnosis.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 3.1 CMD_STATUS at 0x004; Appendix A LTSSM states
dependencies: SerDes locked; Proper partner present; Correct clock configuration
Remarks: Time-bound the wait for L0 and surface errors to upper layers

feature_name: Debug and port logic visibility (DEBUG0/1, PL_GEN2)
description: DEBUG registers expose LTSSM state and other link diagnostics. Port Logic includes timers and Gen2 parameters that affect signal conditioning. These knobs assist in tuning bring-up in marginal environments. Counters for ACK/NACK and replay help assess link quality. Access should be limited to trusted code in production.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 3.9 Port Logic; DEBUG0/DEBUG1; PL_GEN2
dependencies: Access to application/port logic registers; Stable link for meaningful stats
Remarks: Snapshot debug counters before and after stress tests for comparison

feature_name: End-of-interrupt acknowledge via IRQ_EOI
description: Interrupt service completion requires writing the IRQ_EOI register with the event number. This model prevents lost or repeated interrupts. Each event source has a defined number mapping. EOI must be written after clearing the source status. Incorrect EOI ordering can cause interrupt storms.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 3.1 IRQ_EOI at 0x050; Interrupt block mapping
dependencies: Interrupt controller routing; Correct event number usage; Memory barriers
Remarks: Provide a common ISR epilogue that performs EOI consistently

feature_name: Global TLP attributes: Relaxed Ordering and No Snoop
description: TLPCFG allows setting Relaxed Ordering and No Snoop bits for outbound traffic. These attributes influence ordering guarantees and cache coherency across the fabric. Enabling them can reduce latency or bus pressure for suitable workloads. Misuse can violate data dependencies or cache coherency. Drivers must set them only when end-to-end correctness is provable.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 3.1 TLPCFG at 0x010
dependencies: Workload analysis; Platform coherency model; Partner acceptance of attributes
Remarks: Default to conservative settings; enable per-queue or per-transfer as needed

feature_name: Privilege and priority control
description: The PRIORITY register configures master privilege ID and request priority. This helps arbitrate between competing DMA and CPU traffic. Lower numbers represent higher priority. Misconfiguration can starve lower-priority agents. Proper QoS planning balances latency-sensitive and bulk transfers.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 3.1 PRIORITY at 0x03C
dependencies: Bus fabric supports prioritization; System QoS policy defined
Remarks: Document PRIQ/PRIVID allocations for auditability

feature_name: Completion timeout capability (DEV_CAP2)
description: Device Capabilities 2 advertise completion timeout support. Software can enable and react to completion timeout events accordingly. Timeouts highlight stalled or unreachable targets. Policy may include retry, link reset, or error escalation. Proper logging aids root cause analysis.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 3.7 DEV_CAP2 at 0x1094
dependencies: Capability enabled; Interrupt routing for timeout; Timers configured
Remarks: Correlate timeouts with port-level link state to avoid misdiagnosis

feature_name: Root Complex initialization sequence (canonical)
description: A defined sequence prepares clocks, power, and SerDes before enabling LTSSM. After training to L0, enumeration discovers and configures downstream devices. Retry responses can be forced until software is ready to accept inbound accesses. Interrupts and address translations are programmed before enabling traffic. This sequence ensures deterministic and safe bring-up.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 2.11 Operations (RC initialization)
dependencies: PSC/SerDes ready; Proper config of BARs and translations; IRQs masked/unmasked
Remarks: Automate with a state machine to manage error paths and timeouts

feature_name: Inbound/outbound DMA-style data movement semantics
description: Inbound PCIe TLPs are translated and issued on the internal master port to targets. Outbound requests originate on the slave port and are translated to PCIe memory TLPs. Buffering and credit management are handled by the core. Software must provision descriptors or copy engines externally if needed. Ordering and completion semantics follow PCIe rules and platform fabric constraints.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 2 (Architecture data movement); Chapter 1 (Credit management)
dependencies: Address translations in place; Fabric targets ready; Adequate buffering in system
Remarks: Verify write posting and read completion ordering for your workload

feature_name: Unsupported: inbound I/O accesses in RC or EP modes
description: Inbound I/O transactions are not supported by the module. Systems must avoid mapping I/O space to reach the device as an EP. RC should prefer memory space for downstream devices. Attempting inbound I/O can lead to errors or dropped transactions. Design all interactions through memory space windows.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 1 (Not supported list)
dependencies: System design avoids inbound I/O; Use memory BARs instead
Remarks: Audit device trees and BIOS settings to ensure no I/O BARs are assigned

feature_name: Max payload sizes and max remote read request size
description: The implementation supports 128-byte outbound and 256-byte inbound payloads. The maximum remote read request size is 256 bytes. These limits impact throughput and latency for large transfers. Software should program compatible Max Payload and Max Read Request settings during enumeration. Mismatches may throttle performance unnecessarily.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 1 (Max payloads and MRRS)
dependencies: Capability negotiation; Partner device settings; Enumeration code
Remarks: Align MRRS/MPP with switch and endpoint capabilities to avoid fragmenting

feature_name: Single Virtual Channel, Traffic Class, and single-function EP
description: Only one Virtual Channel and one Traffic Class are implemented. EP mode exposes a single PCIe function. This simplifies arbitration and resource management. However, it limits QoS isolation across traffic classes. Software must multiplex flows over the single TC/VC available.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 1 (Virtualization/traffic)
dependencies: None beyond standard PCIe capabilities
Remarks: Use software scheduling to emulate QoS for multiple flows if needed

feature_name: Unsupported features: split x2 to two x1, multiple VCs/TCs, FLR, beacon/in-band wake, vendor-defined messaging, L2 link state
description: Several optional PCIe features are not implemented in this core. These include splitting a x2 link into two independent x1 ports and multi-VC/TC support. Function-level reset and certain wake or vendor-defined messaging mechanisms are absent. L2 link state is also not supported. System design must not rely on these features.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 1 (Not supported list)
dependencies: System architecture chooses alternatives; Firmware avoids referencing unsupported caps
Remarks: Gate feature probing to avoid enabling unsupported capabilities

feature_name: Remote configuration access via CFG_SETUP window
description: RC software can target a specific bus/device/function for Type 0/1 config transactions. The CFG_SETUP register programs the BDF and request type. Accesses must be 32-bit and non-burst. Drivers should avoid touching this window until link and targets are ready. Misuse can cause completion timeouts or secondary bus errors.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Chapter 3.1 CFG_SETUP at 0x008; Chapter 2.8 (Remote Config Window)
dependencies: RC mode; Valid BDF; Link trained to L0
Remarks: Block accesses with APP_RETRY_EN until software is ready to service them
